Ambev’s latest earnings call struck a cautiously upbeat tone as management balanced resilient profitability with pronounced volume headwinds. Executives underscored margin expansion, robust cash generation, and booming digital platforms as proof the strategy is working, even as bad weather, softer demand in core segments, and FX losses weighed on overall performance.

Consolidated Margins and Earnings Power

Ambev delivered another year of margin expansion, with consolidated normalized EBITDA margin up 50 bps to 33.4% and Brazil Beer improving by about 110 bps. Normalized EBITDA grew across the portfolio, supporting stated net income of roughly BRL 16 billion as stated EPS rose 8.2% year on year and normalized EPS increased 2%.

Pricing, Mix, and Revenue Per Hectoliter

Net revenue per hectoliter climbed 7.5% year on year, reflecting tighter revenue management and a clear tilt toward stronger brands and premium offerings. This pricing and mix discipline helped offset weak volumes and underpinned the margin gains seen in both the consolidated business and the Brazil Beer unit.

BEES Marketplace: Scale and Profitability

The BEES B2B marketplace remained a standout growth engine, posting a 70% jump in full‑year GMV. Importantly, BEES gross margin improved by 3.5 percentage points, signaling that the platform is not just scaling but also becoming structurally more profitable for Ambev and its trade partners.

Zé Delivery: Direct Reach to Younger Consumers

Zé Delivery continued to deepen Ambev’s connection with end consumers, particularly younger cohorts. The platform generated BRL 4.7 billion in GMV, up 13% year on year, processed 67 million orders, and grew yearly active users 11% to 27 million, with about 80% of buyers coming from Gen Z and millennial segments.

Premium, Balanced, and Nonalcohol Portfolio Wins

Ambev’s portfolio strategy paid off where consumers traded up or across categories, with premium and super premium volumes growing in the high teens. Balanced‑choice brands surged in the high‑60% range, while nonalcohol volumes advanced about 30% and in the fourth quarter the company captured an estimated 100% of Brazilian beer industry growth in premium and nonalcohol.

Cash Generation and Aggressive Shareholder Returns

Operating cash flow reached BRL 24.5 billion, giving Ambev ample firepower for both investment and capital returns. Management returned BRL 21.7 billion to shareholders on a cash basis, roughly 90% of operating cash flow, and laid out a 2025 program of about BRL 20 billion in dividends, interest on capital, and share buybacks.

Cost Discipline and Operational Efficiency

Cost control remained a key pillar as Brazil Beer cash COGS per hectoliter, excluding marketplace products, rose 6.1% yet still landed in the lowest quartile of prior guidance. Productivity initiatives and industrial and logistics efficiencies helped, while SG&A growth stayed contained even as Ambev continued to fund marketing and brand support.

Volume Headwinds and Industry Weakness

Despite solid pricing and mix, Ambev faced broad‑based volume pressure across its footprint, driven largely by cyclical and occasion‑related factors. Prolonged cold weather linked to La Niña made October particularly weak, and although trends improved in the fourth quarter and December, full‑year volumes remained under strain.

Core Beer Segment Under Pressure

The core beer segment felt the brunt of the downturn due to its heavy exposure to out‑of‑home consumption and social occasions. This dynamic caused share pressure earlier in the year and forced Ambev to ramp up trade activation and marketing efforts, which helped recover share into the fourth quarter but did not fully offset the earlier drag.

Brazil NAB: Profitability Versus Market Share

In Brazil’s non‑alcoholic beverages, management leaned into revenue management decisions that supported profitability but slowed volume momentum. As a result, the NAB business suffered volume deceleration in the second half and some market share pressure versus its stronger first‑half performance.

FX Losses and Higher Net Financial Expenses

Below the operating line, Ambev’s results were hit by nearly BRL 4 billion in net financial expenses for the year. The increase was driven largely by foreign‑exchange variation losses on foreign‑currency‑denominated assets and higher costs linked to sourcing U.S. dollars in Bolivia.

Working Capital Drag on Cash Flow

While cash generation stayed strong overall, cash flow from operating activities fell BRL 1.6 billion year on year to BRL 24.5 billion, mainly due to softer volumes pressuring working capital. Financing activities consumed BRL 26.8 billion, reflecting heavy shareholder payouts and the completion of a previous share buyback program.

Regional Soft Spots: Argentina and Dominican Republic

Not all geographies participated equally in the recovery narrative, with Argentina’s consumption rebound moving slower than management had hoped despite sequential improvement. The Dominican Republic also saw a setback in the fourth quarter as adverse weather disrupted performance, adding to the broader volume headwinds.

2026 Outlook: Costs Up, Margins Still in Focus

Looking ahead, Ambev guided Brazil Beer cash COGS per hectoliter, excluding non‑Ambev marketplace products, to increase 4.5% to 7.5% in 2026 on commodity, aluminum, and mix pressures. Even so, management reiterated a commitment to expand consolidated EBITDA margins over time, supported by disciplined capital allocation, ongoing digital growth, and continued revenue per hectoliter gains.

Ambev’s earnings call painted a picture of a brewer that is absorbing meaningful external shocks yet still protecting profitability and returning substantial cash to shareholders. Investors will now watch whether easing weather and macro conditions, combined with the premium and digital strategies, can translate into a clearer volume recovery as the company navigates another year of cost headwinds.

data.title: Liberty Broadband Charts Growth Amid Heavy 2026 CapEx
data.description:
Liberty Broadband Corp ((LBRDA)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Liberty Broadband’s latest earnings call struck a cautiously optimistic tone, with management emphasizing record revenue, record adjusted OIBDA, and sharply higher free cash flow alongside a strong cash position after a successful rights offering. These financial gains and a clear roadmap for network upgrades and rural expansion were weighed against subscriber losses, outage-related challenges, and a temporary drag from elevated capital spending.

Record Revenue in a Challenging Demand Environment

Liberty Broadband reported record full‑year revenue of $1.0 billion, up 3% year over year, underscoring steady top‑line growth despite competitive and technology headwinds. Fourth‑quarter revenue was flat at $262 million versus the prior year, signaling that momentum slowed late in the year as data subscriber declines offset gains elsewhere.

OIBDA Hits New Highs, Underscoring Operating Leverage

Adjusted OIBDA reached a record $403 million for the year, a robust 12% increase that outpaced revenue growth and highlighted strong cost discipline and mix improvements. In the fourth quarter, adjusted OIBDA rose 7% to $90 million, suggesting that the company continues to find efficiencies even as it invests heavily in its network.

Free Cash Flow Surges on Strong Results and Working Capital

Free cash flow jumped to $146 million for the year, an increase of more than 70% compared with 2024, driven by record profitability and favorable working capital swings. This surge gives Liberty Broadband additional flexibility to fund its aggressive investment program while managing leverage and maintaining liquidity.

Rights Offering Bolsters Balance Sheet and Liquidity

The company completed a fully subscribed rights offering that generated roughly $300 million in net proceeds, significantly strengthening its cash position. Consolidated cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash totaled $429 million at year‑end, providing a sizable buffer as capital expenditures ramp and strategic opportunities arise.

Business Segment Delivers Growth and High Margins

Business services remained a bright spot, with revenue up 7% for the full year and 1% in the fourth quarter as enterprise demand held firm. Business gross margin expanded to 80.1% for the year, and 78.3% in Q4, helped by a strong upgrade cycle that is pushing more customers to higher‑value connectivity solutions.

Wireless and Bundling Strategy Gain Traction

Consumer wireless lines grew about 2% year over year to 199,000, and total wireless lines, including business, reached 207,500, reflecting steady adoption. Postpaid lines peaked at 165,400 and 62% of those lines are now sold in bundles, up from 57% a year earlier, underscoring management’s convergence strategy to deepen customer relationships.

Network Upgrades Target Higher Speeds and Capacity

Liberty Broadband is advancing DOCSIS 4.0‑capable HFC and wireless upgrades, already offering 2.5 Gbps in areas with fiber middle mile in place. In Anchorage, the company is upgrading its 1.8 GHz core and reducing node sizes, laying the groundwork to scale speeds to 5 Gbps and beyond and positioning the network for future data demand.

Alaska Build‑outs and BEAD Awards Support Expansion

The company completed its iHUC one net build‑out to deliver 2.5 Gbps service to Delta, marking a key milestone in its Alaska expansion plan. It has also been provisionally awarded about $120 million in BEAD funds by the State of Alaska, which, once approved, would offset a significant portion of the capital costs of extending broadband to underserved areas.

Operational Resilience Tested by Extreme Events

Management highlighted rapid restoration efforts after major outages, including repairing a Dutch Harbor fiber break in less than two weeks and fully restoring Typhoon Helong‑impacted villages in under four months. Fiber service on a third‑party network was also fully restored during 2025, demonstrating the company’s capability to respond to severe disruptions, albeit at a cost.

Data Subscriber Losses Reflect Intensifying Competition

Data subscribers fell 3% year over year to 151,200, with a loss of 4,500 subscribers over the year and 1,200 in the fourth quarter, underscoring churn pressures. Management cited wireless substitution, competition from providers like Starlink, and fallout from a third‑party fiber break as key drivers of the decline.

Consumer Revenue Faces Headwinds Despite Wireless Growth

Consumer revenue declined 2% for the full year as the shutdown of the legacy video business and data subscriber losses weighed on the top line. These pressures were only partly offset by growth in wireless revenue and higher federal wireless subsidies, highlighting the importance of converged offerings to stabilize consumer results.

Service Interruptions Add Cost and Reputation Risk

December brought two significant fiber breaks in Dutch Harbor and Dearing, with Dutch Harbor resolved quickly but Dearing repairs pushed to summer due to ice conditions. Management expects repair costs in the low single‑digit millions, adding near‑term expense and raising the bar for customer retention in affected markets.

Promotions Power Postpaid Growth but Raise Questions

Postpaid wireless growth was materially driven by promotions, including an unlimited test drive and a twelve‑month free offer, which successfully boosted line counts. Early retention trends are described as strong, but the dependence on heavy promotions, alongside ongoing prepaid and lifeline erosion, suggests that sustainable organic growth remains a work in progress.

BEAD Funding Still Subject to Approval and Timing Risk

While the provisional BEAD award of roughly $120 million is a potential tailwind, it remains subject to federal approval and state negotiations that could affect timing and size. This uncertainty complicates capital planning for the expansion program, even as management assumes that grants will ultimately reduce the out‑of‑pocket spend on rural projects.

CapEx Peak in 2026 to Pressure Near‑Term Free Cash Flow

Management flagged 2026 as a peak investment year, with expected capital expenditures of about $290 million, including roughly $20 million carried over from 2025, versus 2025 CapEx of $224 million net of grants. The company signaled that this elevated spend will drive proportionately lower free cash flow in 2026, before dropping back toward a more normal 15–20% of revenue range longer term.

Leverage Manageable but Under Watch as Spending Rises

Total debt stands at about $1.0 billion, with net leverage under the credit agreement at 2.3x and consolidated net leverage at 1.6x after factoring in parent cash and preferred stock. These metrics remain reasonable, yet investors will be watching closely as CapEx ramps to ensure the balance sheet stays flexible and interest costs remain contained.

Customer Win‑Back After Outages Proves Slow

Despite restoration of third‑party fiber service, management acknowledged that winning back customers in outage‑affected areas has been slower than hoped, signaling lingering trust and switching‑cost issues. This slow recovery suggests that certain markets could face longer‑term revenue drag, making reliability and communication key priorities alongside network upgrades.

Guidance Points to Stable 2026 Amid Investment Cycle

Looking ahead, management characterized 2026 as “stable,” built on the foundation of record 2025 results, ample cash of $429 million, roughly $1.0 billion of debt, and $377 million in undrawn revolver capacity. With 2026 CapEx projected at around $290 million, the company expects lower free cash flow next year as it funds DOCSIS 4.0 and 5G‑ready upgrades, completes Alaska build‑outs, and selectively deploys rights‑offering proceeds, while aiming for a return to more normalized investment levels over time.

Liberty Broadband’s earnings call painted a picture of a company in the middle of a heavy but purposeful investment cycle, balancing solid current profitability with ambitious network upgrades and rural expansion. Record financial metrics, strong liquidity, and promising BEAD grants provide support, but investors must weigh these positives against subscriber losses, outage‑related challenges, and a temporary dip in free cash flow as CapEx peaks in 2026.

ZoomInfo Technologies, Inc. delivered an earnings call that mixed solid profitability and cash generation with tempered growth expectations and clear market headwinds. Management emphasized disciplined execution, strong margins, and aggressive capital returns, while openly acknowledging sluggish top‑line growth, downmarket pressure, SEO challenges, and rising interest costs that could cap near‑term upside.

Record Q4 Revenue and Strong Profitability

ZoomInfo posted Q4 revenue of $319M, up 3% year over year, underscoring stable but unspectacular growth in a cautious demand environment. Adjusted operating income hit $123M for a robust 38% margin, allowing management to claim a return to “rule of 40” performance even as sales momentum remained modest.

Full‑Year 2025 Financial Performance

For 2025, revenue reached $1.25B, also up 3% versus the prior year, reinforcing the theme of steady but slow expansion. Profitability stayed a key strength, with adjusted operating income of $446M at a 36% margin and adjusted unlevered free cash flow of $455M, while levered free cash flow per share rose 12% to $1.20.

Strong Free Cash Flow and Capital Returns

The company leaned hard into shareholder returns, buying back 40.5M shares for $407M in 2025, roughly 12% of its share count, and authorizing another $1B in repurchases, close to half its market cap. Q4 GAAP operating cash flow jumped 30% to $143M and unlevered free cash flow of $135M represented a 42% margin and 110% conversion, underscoring the business’s cash‑rich profile.

Upmarket Momentum and Customer Mix Shift

ZoomInfo continued to pivot upmarket, where Q4 revenue grew 6% year over year, triple last year’s pace for the seasonally strongest quarter, and management expects upmarket to reach 80% of mix by late 2027. Upmarket now represents 74% of the business, with 1,921 customers above $100k in ACV, a growing cohort of $1M‑plus accounts, and large enterprises now exceeding half of total company ACV.

AI Platform Adoption — Copilot Traction

AI‑driven offerings were a highlight as Copilot more than doubled in 2025 and now accounts for over 20% of total ACV, with penetration nearing one‑fifth of overall contracts and even higher within the SalesOS base. Early renewals show better rates and uplift versus legacy tools, and management said migration to Copilot is tracking ahead of plan, suggesting AI will be a key driver of future wallet share.

Operations / Data‑as‑a‑Service Growth

The operations and data‑as‑a‑service segment stood out with more than 20% year‑over‑year growth in the quarter, making it nearly one‑fifth of total ACV and a rare pocket of double‑digit expansion. ZoomInfo also continued to invest in data scale and quality, adding over 10M contacts and broadening coverage across six European markets to support multinational go‑to‑market strategies.

Product Innovation and Go‑to‑Market Orchestration

Management highlighted a flurry of product innovation, including GTM Studio, the AI‑native GTM Workspace for sellers, MCP server integrations, and deeper hooks into Salesforce, HubSpot, and Microsoft Dynamics. Case studies from customers like monday.com, Hilton, Edward Jones, Kaseya, and several Fortune 500 wins were used to showcase real‑world productivity gains and validate the broader go‑to‑market orchestration vision.

Improving Expense Quality Metrics

Expense quality improved as stock‑based compensation fell below 10% of revenue for the year, a key point for investors focused on dilution and earnings quality. The company is shifting toward performance‑based equity plans, which management framed as better aligned with shareholder value creation and supportive of sustainable margin strength.

Conservative but Positive 2026 Financial Guide

Management issued a cautious but profitable outlook, guiding Q1 2026 revenue to $306–309M and full‑year revenue to $1.247–1.267B, roughly 1% growth at the midpoint. For 2026 they projected adjusted operating income of $456–466M at about a 37% margin and unlevered free cash flow of $435–465M, while calling out seasonally lower Q1 margins, higher cash interest, and the exclusion of revenue from new products in the forecast.

Low Top‑Line Growth and Decelerating Guidance

Despite strong profitability, revenue growth remained subdued at 3% for both Q4 and FY2025, and the 2026 guide implies only about 1% growth, signaling a deceleration relative to past expectations. Management characterized the stance as deliberately conservative given mixed demand signals and ongoing headwinds, but the numbers underline that the story is currently more about earnings and cash than expansion.

Net Revenue Retention Behind Peak

Net revenue retention held at 90% in the quarter, flat versus Q3 and well below prior peaks, reflecting limited expansion and some contraction in parts of the base. While upmarket NRR is around 100% and improving, overall NRR stagnation despite enterprise renewals remains a concern for investors looking for signs of re‑acceleration.

Downmarket Weakness

The downmarket segment continued to struggle, contracting roughly 10% year over year for the second straight quarter and now accounting for about 26% of the mix. Management suggested this pressure will persist into 2026 and baked continued downmarket weakness into guidance, reinforcing the strategic emphasis on larger, stickier customers.

Calculated Billings and Bookings Volatility

Calculated billings were flat for the year and current calculated bookings grew only mid‑single digits, offering little evidence of a strong near‑term re‑acceleration. Executives urged caution in interpreting these metrics due to changes in billing terms and reserve dynamics, effectively asking investors to focus more on cash generation and upmarket trends than noisy billing data.

Exposure to Interest Costs and Leverage

ZoomInfo closed the year with about $1.3B in gross debt against roughly $180M in cash, translating to net leverage of about 2.4x trailing EBITDA, a manageable but notable level. With a key interest rate swap now expired, the company expects cash interest expenses of $60–65M in 2026 tied to variable SOFR rates, a headwind that will modestly eat into otherwise strong free cash flow.

SEO and Top‑of‑Funnel Headwinds

Search and SEO disruptions, including AI‑driven changes in how prospects discover tools, have hurt top‑of‑funnel demand, and traffic has yet to return to prior levels. Management said the negative impact has eased slightly but remains a challenge, prompting continued investment to rebuild organic pipeline even as lower inbound volume weighs on growth.

One‑Time and Structural Costs

The company recorded real estate impairments tied to office space in Vancouver and Renanah and expects restructuring‑related cash outflows in 2026 to fund tenant improvements for sublets. Executives also warned that Q1 margins will be seasonally lower due to payroll tax and benefit resets, with profitability expected to improve as the year progresses.

Guidance Excludes Revenue from New Products

In a notable conservative choice, ZoomInfo’s 2026 revenue guide excludes any contribution from newly launched products like GTM Studio and related offerings, even though their associated operating costs are fully included. This approach lowers near‑term expectations but potentially sets up room for upside if new platforms gain traction faster than modeled.

Forward‑Looking Outlook and Guidance Takeaways

Looking ahead, management is signaling a year of muted revenue growth but sustained high margins and strong cash generation, with 2026 AOI margins expected to rise to about 37% and free cash flow remaining substantial despite higher interest costs. The company framed its guidance as prudently conservative, highlighted potential upside from AI and new products not yet in the numbers, and reiterated that capital returns via buybacks will stay a key lever for driving per‑share value.

ZoomInfo’s earnings call painted a picture of a company leaning on efficiency, product innovation, and aggressive buybacks to offset a tough demand environment and limited near‑term growth. Investors will now watch closely to see whether upmarket expansion, AI‑driven platforms, and operations‑focused products can eventually translate strong profitability into a re‑accelerating top line.

Rithm Property Trust’s latest earnings call struck a cautiously optimistic tone, mixing flat near-term results with an asset-rich balance sheet and a detailed plan for future growth. Management emphasized liquidity, a strong origination pipeline, and identified accretive assets, but acknowledged that the roadmap depends heavily on favorable market conditions for a major recapitalization.

Solid Liquidity and Equity Base

Rithm Property Trust ended the quarter with about $100 million of cash and liquidity and total equity of roughly $300 million. This capital base gives the company flexibility to pursue opportunistic investments and positions it to act quickly if markets open a window for recapitalization.

Book Value Strength vs. Market Discount

Book value stands at approximately $300 million, or around $31 per diluted share, underscoring the company’s tangible asset backing. Yet the stock trades near $15–$16, roughly 50% of book, signaling that public markets remain skeptical despite the underlying balance sheet strength.

Soft Q4 Earnings and Negative Adjusted EPS

For the fourth quarter, Rithm Property Trust reported GAAP earnings of $2.5 million, but after adjustments EAD came in at about –$0.5 million. That translated into a diluted loss per share of roughly –$0.06, highlighting that core earnings momentum remains muted in the absence of new capital deployment.

Attractive Dividend Yield in a Weak Tape

The company continues to pay a common dividend that equates to an indicated yield of about 8.7% at current prices. Management’s long-term vision assumes the stock could ultimately trade around a 9% dividend yield, making the payout central to the broader recapitalization and valuation thesis.

Reverse Split Aims to Broaden Investor Base

Management completed a 6-to-1 reverse share split, lifting the stock from roughly $2 pre-split to about $15–$16 today. The move is designed to improve market perception, reduce “penny stock” stigma, and attract a wider set of institutional and retail investors to the Rithm Property Trust story.

Explosive Origination Growth at Genesis

A key growth engine is Genesis, where origination volume is projected at roughly $6–$7 billion this year versus $1.7 billion in 2022. That represents a roughly 250%–312% increase and creates a sizable, internally sourced pipeline of loans that can be selectively added to Rithm’s balance sheet.

Pipeline of Accretive Loan Opportunities

Management has identified about $1 billion of loans and assets that could be transferred onto Rithm Property Trust’s balance sheet, pending board approval. These loans are expected to be immediately accretive with no J-curve, offering a direct path to higher earnings once capital is available.

Participation in Paramount Acquisition

Rithm Property Trust holds roughly $50 million in the Paramount transaction, a larger deal of about $1.6 billion with net operating income near $300 million. The position entitles Rithm to a proportional slice of the cash flows, adding another income stream tied to institutional-scale real estate.

Broad Sourcing and Capital Distribution Network

Beyond Genesis and Paramount, Rithm and its affiliates tap multiple origination and partnership channels, including third-party originators, funds and separately managed accounts. The company has also launched fund distribution on a wirehouse platform, supporting the ability to scale and attract third-party capital.

Ambitious Earnings Targets Post-Recapitalization

Management outlined a pathway, contingent on a recapitalization, to grow earnings to roughly $1.60–$1.70 per share. Under those assumptions, Rithm Property Trust envisions an implied book value nearer $20 per share, aligning a higher earnings base with a more normalized dividend yield and trading multiple.

Flat Near-Term Performance Limits Momentum

Quarterly results remain essentially flat, with modest GAAP profit offset by negative adjusted earnings of about –$0.06 per diluted share. Without a meaningful capital raise or significant asset deployment, near-term earnings are unlikely to break out, keeping performance in a holding pattern.

Recapitalization as Critical Swing Factor

The company’s path to higher EPS and a tighter discount to book is heavily dependent on executing a sizable recapitalization and forming new capital. Management stressed that timing and pricing are market-driven, introducing notable execution risk around when, and on what terms, Rithm can scale its balance sheet.

Deep Discount Reflects Market Skepticism

Despite its $31 per share book value, Rithm Property Trust’s stock changes hands at roughly half that level. This deep discount not only reflects investor caution around commercial real estate but also limits the firm’s willingness to issue common equity as a growth currency at current valuations.

Muted Investment Activity in the Quarter

New investment activity in Q4 was deliberately light, apart from the incremental Paramount exposure. While this conservatism protects capital amid volatility, it also caps immediate earnings upside, placing more weight on future deployment once conditions improve.

Sector Headwinds and Volatile Markets

Management pointed to a dislocated commercial real estate market and broader weakness across REITs and BDCs, amplified by macro and AI-driven trading flows. These headwinds complicate the timing of capital raises and asset sales or purchases, making patience and selectivity essential.

Balancing Dilution Risk with Growth Needs

Leaders signaled reluctance to issue common equity while the stock trades at a steep discount to book, prioritizing the avoidance of highly dilutive deals. Alternative structures, such as preferred or third-party capital, are on the table, but until conditions improve, balance sheet expansion will likely remain measured.

Guidance and Roadmap Hinges on Market Window

Looking ahead, management’s guidance centers on a scenario where a successful recapitalization unlocks EPS of roughly $1.60–$1.70, a dividend yield near 9%, and book value around $20 per share. Supporting that vision, the company highlighted its $100 million liquidity, $300 million equity base, $1 billion of potential balance sheet assets, and a Genesis pipeline that could reach $6–$7 billion in originations.

Rithm Property Trust’s call painted a picture of solid assets and strategic optionality set against soft near-term earnings and a skeptical market. Investors will be watching closely to see if management can navigate sector headwinds, unlock the identified loan pipeline, and execute a recapitalization that turns today’s deep discount into tomorrow’s upside rerating.

Schroders (SDR) struck an upbeat tone on its latest earnings call, balancing record financial results with a transformative takeover proposal from Nuveen. Management stressed strong profit growth, improved efficiency and healthier flows, while acknowledging pockets of weakness in Wealth, private markets and China. Overall, the message was one of solid progress and strategic acceleration despite macro and market headwinds.

Strong profitability and EPS growth

Operating profit rose 25% year‑on‑year, with adjusted operating EPS up an even faster 29%. Management linked this to better operating leverage and tangible benefits from its multi‑year transformation programme, signalling that Schroders is now converting past cost and platform investments into bottom‑line gains.

Record AUM and net inflows

Assets under management hit a record £824 billion, up 6% over the year, underscoring the resilience of the franchise. Schroders generated £142 billion of gross inflows, up 9%, and delivered £11.2 billion of net inflows, confirming that overall client demand remains positive despite selective outflows.

Nuveen’s all‑cash offer and valuation

The headline news was Nuveen’s proposed all‑cash offer of up to £6.12 per share, including permitted dividends. The deal values Schroders at roughly 17 times 2025 fully diluted adjusted operating EPS and builds in chunky premiums of 34% to the prior close, 47% to the 3‑month VWAP and 61% to the 12‑month VWAP.

Strategic scale from the proposed combination

If completed, the tie‑up would create a global asset and wealth manager with more than $1.8 trillion of combined AUM. The enlarged group would manage about £1.3 trillion in Public Markets and £307 billion in Private Markets, with complementary geographic footprints that strengthen Schroders’ historically smaller U.S. presence and broaden its product range.

Revenue growth and segment performance

Adjusted operating income increased 6%, with most segments contributing. Asset Management net operating revenue grew 4%, Public Markets 5% and Schroders Capital 3%, while Wealth stood out with 10% net operating revenue growth and a 12% rise in adjusted operating revenue, translating into a three‑year compound annual growth rate of 15%.

Recovery in net new revenue momentum

Net new revenue momentum swung into positive territory, showing early benefits from product and distribution initiatives. Annualised net new revenue moved from a negative £46 million in 2024 to a positive £15 million in 2025, and Public Markets flows improved sharply with net new business shifting from a £21 billion outflow to a £3.7 billion inflow.

Fundraising strength and dry powder

Schroders Capital kept fundraising steady at £10.9 billion, matching last year despite a tougher backdrop. Fee‑earning dry powder rose by £0.7 billion to £4.9 billion, giving the business significant capital to deploy into future private‑market opportunities as market conditions and client demand allow.

Transformation delivery and cost savings

The transformation programme is now visibly contributing to earnings, with £75 million of in‑year net savings after reinvestment. Annualised net savings are around £100 million, helped by a 12% reduction in the supplier base and a 10% cut in headcount, and management is targeting £150 million of net annualised savings by 2027.

Improved operating leverage and margins

Operating leverage continued to improve, with the cost‑to‑income ratio declining to 71%. Management aims to move towards 70% in 2026 and to break below 70% in 2027, while equity margins exited the year at 44 basis points and fixed‑income margins also strengthened on exit, reflecting better pricing and mix.

Product innovation and distribution gains

On the product side, Schroders launched active UCITS ETFs in Europe and has already built more than $1 billion in assets within just four months. The group expanded Schroders Capital’s sales force by 40 specialists and reignited intermediary momentum, with intermediary flows rising to more than £4.5 billion from under £3 billion the year before.

Employee and talent dynamics

The firm highlighted significant leadership refreshment, with 15% of leaders joining from outside and 26% promoted internally, leaving more than 40% in new roles. Employee engagement looks stable, as Schroders retained over 95% of staff who received the highest performance ratings, supporting continuity through its transformation and potential combination with Nuveen.

Schroders Capital deployment challenge

Despite healthy fundraising, Schroders Capital’s net new business was softer than hoped, revealing a gap between capital raised and capital deployed. Management conceded the need to accelerate investment of the £4.9 billion of fee‑earning dry powder, since converting this into invested assets is key to unlocking higher revenue and fee margins.

Wealth Management flows below ambition

Wealth Management delivered strong revenue growth but fell short on flow targets, with a net new business run‑rate of 2.7%, below its ambition. The business was hit by charity drawdowns and outflows in low‑margin mandates, muting overall flows even as top‑line revenues rose by 10%.

Joint venture and regional outflows

Joint ventures were a notable drag, posting £5 billion of outflows largely from the Chinese fund management partnership. In addition, Schroders saw outflows in regional equity strategies and Asian bonds and lost some low‑margin mandates, though multi‑asset outflows from prior large mandate losses moderated compared with 2024.

FX, inflation and short‑term cost pressures

External factors weighed on results, with a weaker U.S. dollar cutting net operating income by £28 million and inflation, FX and AUM‑linked items adding £54 million to expenses. Unexpected building repair costs of £20 million and reinvestment of £19 million into growth meant adjusted operating expenses were flat year‑on‑year despite underlying efficiency gains.

Transformation reinvestment and phased savings

Management stressed that transformation savings will not all drop through immediately because some are being recycled into growth initiatives and platform upgrades. Ongoing technology and platform implementation work means that a portion of the cost benefits will be phased over several years, smoothing the path towards the 2027 savings target.

Capital requirements and regulatory uncertainty

Schroders said it has provisioned for an estimated £250 million impact from Basel 3.1, leaving a capital surplus of £865 million today. However, ongoing discussions with the regulator introduce some uncertainty around final capital requirements, which could influence future capital deployment and shareholder return decisions.

Reliance on supportive markets

Management acknowledged that strong markets and performance fees have provided a helpful tailwind to margins and profits. They cautioned that weaker markets could reverse some of this benefit, underlining that a portion of the recent margin expansion is cyclical rather than purely structural, even as transformation savings should partially offset downturns.

Pre‑transaction geographic and product gaps

Before the Nuveen proposal, Schroders recognised that its U.S. distribution footprint was relatively modest and that it lacked scale in certain private‑market strategies. These gaps were cited as key drivers for pursuing the deal, which would instantly add scale in the U.S. and broaden the combined group’s private‑market capabilities.

Guidance and outlook

Looking ahead, management is targeting completion of the Nuveen all‑cash offer around the fourth quarter of 2026, which would create an over $1.8 trillion AUM group with £307 billion in Private Markets. Operationally, Schroders aims for £150 million of net annualised savings by 2027, a cost‑to‑income ratio below 70%, and continued growth in AUM, fundraising and intermediary flows off the strong 2025 base, all subject to market conditions and regulatory outcomes.

Schroders’ earnings call painted a picture of a business delivering on profitability, efficiency and innovation while moving to resolve scale and geographic gaps through the proposed Nuveen deal. While challenges remain in Wealth flows, private‑market deployment, China‑related outflows and regulatory capital, the overall narrative was positive, with management positioning the firm for a larger and more efficient future platform.

data.title: Flex LNG Balances Strong 2025 Results With Cautious Outlook
data.description:
Flex Lng ((FLNG)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Flex LNG’s latest earnings call struck a cautiously upbeat tone, with management highlighting strong 2025 financial results, healthy cash reserves, and high utilization, while warning that a looming wave of new LNG carrier deliveries and volatile spot markets could cap earnings growth. Investors heard a story of solid execution today paired with tempered expectations for tomorrow’s more crowded marketplace.

Strong earnings and profitability metrics

Flex LNG reported adjusted EBITDA of $251 million and adjusted net income of $101 million for 2025, landing squarely within prior guidance and underscoring disciplined commercial and cost management. The results show the company is still extracting attractive returns from its modern fleet even as spot markets softened versus the prior year.

Cash-rich balance sheet with long runway

The company closed the year with a robust $448 million cash balance, no debt maturing before 2029, and a book equity ratio of 27%, which together give Flex LNG substantial financial flexibility. This balance sheet strength offers resilience if spot rates weaken and provides optionality for future investments or capital returns.

Reliable dividends and double-digit yield

The board declared another $0.75 per share quarterly dividend, the 18th consecutive payout at that level, bringing the last 12 months total to $3.00 per share and implying a yield around 11.5%. Since 2021 the company has returned roughly $770 million to shareholders, reinforcing its identity as an income-focused LNG shipping vehicle.

High utilization and operational discipline

Operationally, Flex LNG delivered near-100% technical uptime after dry docks, underscoring strong fleet reliability and vessel management. The company completed 64 dry dock days in 2025 versus a budgeted 80 days, creating additional earning days and supporting revenue despite softer market conditions.

Solid time-charter equivalents despite softness

Time-charter equivalent (TCE) earnings remained strong, at about $70,100 per day in Q4 and roughly $71,700–$72,000 per day for 2025, reflecting the benefit of contracted cover. For 2026 management guided a broader TCE range of $65,000–$75,000 per day, acknowledging rate uncertainty for the portion of the fleet that will trade on the spot market.

Deep contract backlog and charter coverage

Flex LNG emphasized its minimum firm contract backlog equivalent to 50 years of vessel employment, which could expand to as much as 75 years if charter options are exercised. For 2026, about 78% of available days are already fixed on long-term charters, giving the company meaningful earnings visibility even as spot conditions fluctuate.

Hedging locks in low interest costs

The company’s interest rate hedging strategy continues to pay off, with its swap portfolio valued at about $17.5 million and an average fixed rate near 2.5% at roughly 70% hedge ratio into mid-2027. These swaps have generated around $132 million in realized and unrealized gains since early 2021, effectively insulating Flex LNG from higher global financing costs.

Demand strength led by Europe and the U.S.

On the market side, global LNG exports rose about 4% year on year to roughly 429 million tonnes in 2025, while U.S. exports grew around 25% and European imports increased 24%, supporting tonnage demand. This Atlantic-led growth underpins employment for modern LNG carriers and offsets weaker dynamics in parts of Asia.

Long-term contract wins bolster visibility

Management highlighted the Flex Constellation, which is set to complete its final voyage in March before commencing a new 15-year time charter. This long-dated contract adds significant revenue visibility and exemplifies Flex LNG’s strategy of anchoring its fleet with stable, long-term employment.

Stable newbuild prices support asset values

Newbuilding prices for standard modern two-stroke LNG vessels remain around $250 million, which supports the valuation of Flex LNG’s existing high-spec tonnage. Stable asset prices help underpin the company’s net asset value and provide a buffer for shareholders even if short-term freight rates come under pressure.

Softer revenues from spot exposure

Full-year sell-in revenues reached $340 million, but this marked a roughly $15 million decline from the prior year, largely due to more exposure to a weaker spot market for vessels like Flex Constellation and Flex Artemis. The experience illustrates how incremental spot exposure can drag on topline performance when freight markets soften.

Wide 2026 ranges underscore volatility

For 2026 Flex LNG provided wide guidance ranges, with revenues seen between $310 million and $340 million, TCE at $65,000–$75,000 per day, and adjusted EBITDA at $225 million–$255 million. Management attributed this dispersion to the three ships that will be exposed to highly volatile spot conditions and uncertain future fixtures.

Operating costs drifting higher

Operating expenses per day climbed to $16,600 in Q4 and averaged $15,800 per day for 2025, slightly above prior guidance of $15,500. For 2026 the company budgets roughly $16,000 per day, citing scheduled maintenance and inflationary pressures on crew and other costs, which modestly trim margins.

Risks from new vessel deliveries

Management expressed caution about a potential near-term oversupply, with 90–95 new LNG carriers expected in 2026 and an orderbook of about 290 ships, roughly 40% of the existing fleet. Such a large delivery pipeline may increase tonnage supply and put downward pressure on spot charter rates over the next few years.

Charter option outcomes remain a swing factor

Several charter options on ships including Flex Resolute, Flex Courageous, and Flex Freedom will need to be decided in 2026–2027, and outcomes remain uncertain. Whether these options are exercised will influence the company’s future contract coverage and could tilt its exposure more toward secure charters or the spot market.

Mixed Asian demand picture

While Europe has been a demand engine, the company noted reduced momentum in parts of Asia, including a roughly 15% year-on-year decline in China’s LNG imports in 2025. India also remained price-sensitive, pulling back when benchmark prices rose, which adds regional unevenness to what is otherwise a growing global LNG trade.

Management tempers expectations for spot earnings

Given the volatile freight environment and heavy newbuild schedule, Flex LNG signaled only modest earnings expectations from its spot-exposed vessels in 2026. Management explicitly flagged the earnings and cash flow outlook for these ships as more cautious, indicating limited upside unless market conditions improve.

Guidance signals stability with selective risk

Looking ahead to 2026, Flex LNG guided revenues of $310–$340 million, TCE of $65,000–$75,000 per day, and adjusted EBITDA of $225–$255 million, while keeping about 78% of days fixed across 10 vessels on time charters and three ships trading spot. The company plans three dry dockings at roughly $5.9 million each with around 20 days off-hire, maintains $448 million in cash, faces no debt maturities before 2029, and continues its $0.75 per share quarterly dividend and interest-rate hedging program.

Flex LNG’s earnings call painted the picture of a company in strong current shape, backed by firm contracts, ample liquidity, and a generous dividend, yet fully aware of gathering headwinds from a swelling global LNG fleet and patchy Asian demand. For investors, the story is one of attractive present income and solid downside protection, offset by more modest growth prospects until market supply and demand rebalance.

Fingerprint Cards Ab (($SE:FING.B)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Fingerprint Cards AB’s latest earnings call painted a cautiously optimistic picture, with management stressing a clear turnaround trajectory despite lingering risks. Full-year growth, strong margins, and fast-building momentum in the higher-value AllKey platform stood out, even as currency headwinds, a thin cash cushion, and long sales cycles keep execution risk front and center.

Full-Year Revenue Rebound Signals Business Turnaround

Full-year 2025 revenue grew 30% year-on-year, or about 40% in constant currency, indicating that the restructuring efforts are gaining traction. Management framed this as evidence of multi-quarter improvement rather than a one-off, suggesting that the topline recovery is starting to show a more durable pattern.

High Gross Margins Underpin Earnings Quality

The company maintained robust profitability, with Q4 gross margin at 65.8% and full-year gross margin at 60.7%. Management added that current pipeline opportunities generally sit in the 50%–60% gross-margin range, implying that future growth should not come at the expense of pricing power or unit economics.

AllKey Gains Traction and Lifts Revenue per Customer

AllKey, Fingerprint Cards’ new platform, is gaining real momentum, with around half of existing customers now on an upgrade path to it. Crucially, AllKey carries roughly three times the average selling price of traditional sensors, and the pipeline mix is shifting in its favor, setting the stage for richer revenue per device.

Pipeline Expansion and Diversification of Customer Base

The commercial pipeline is broadening, with more than 20% growth expected between 2026 and 2027. Management highlighted that about half of pipeline leads come from new customers, signaling that growth is not just upselling existing accounts but also tapping new segments and partnerships.

AllKey Ultra Opens Door to Higher-Security Markets

In December, the firm launched AllKey Ultra, a secure-element variant targeting high-security use cases such as authentication, payments, crypto wallets, and wearables. This adds differentiation and expands the addressable market, potentially deepening the competitive moat if adoption scales as planned.

Asset Deals and Partnerships Provide Incremental Upside

The company is also monetizing technology via deals and licensing, including a 50/50 revenue-share iris-authentication partnership with Smart Eye and a separate transaction with PixArt closed in Q4. These arrangements showcase the portfolio’s broader value and offer additional funding streams, although they are not yet recurring.

Early Profitability Signals and Tight Cost Discipline

Q4 EBITDA and free cash flow turned slightly positive, a key psychological milestone after a period of restructuring. Headcount is down 31% year-on-year and flat quarter-on-quarter, while management leans on AI tools and partner channels to preserve productivity and operating leverage with a leaner team.

Q4 Revenue Soft Patch Masked by FX Effects

Quarterly revenue dipped 4% year-on-year, reflecting some near-term softness and foreign-exchange pressure. On a constant-currency basis, however, Q4 revenue was up 9%, suggesting underlying demand remains intact even if reported numbers looked weaker.

Thin Cash Cushion Highlights Liquidity Sensitivity

Cash ended the quarter at SEK 27.1 million, down SEK 1.2 million versus Q3, leaving limited room for missteps. This makes the business more sensitive to the timing of customer payments and asset deals and puts a premium on disciplined working-capital management.

Pipeline Conversion and Long Sales Cycles Add Uncertainty

Management underscored that pipeline does not equal signed revenue, with productization and customer design-ins for AllKey and AllKey Ultra typically taking 9–24 months. This long lead time introduces timing risk, meaning reported results may lag the apparent strength of the opportunity set.

Reliance on Episodic Deals and Smaller Workforce

While asset and licensing deals can be meaningful, management described them as episodic rather than predictable, contributing to volatility in near-term cash inflows. At the same time, the 31% headcount reduction supports margins but may constrain capacity unless efficiency gains from AI and channel partners fully compensate.

Market Opportunity Not Guaranteed in All Segments

Some of the more exciting markets, such as smart cards and payments, are still in early stages and may not scale as quickly as hoped. Management explicitly cautioned that demand in these areas is uncertain, reminding investors that upside scenarios remain optional rather than baked into the base case.

Guidance: Stable Fundamentals with Focus on AllKey

Looking ahead, the company signaled stable fundamentals, with full-year revenue growth of 30% (40% in constant currency) and gross margins expected to stay in the 50%–60% range. Management plans to double down on AllKey and asset or licensing deals, with about half of existing customers on the AllKey path and roughly half of the pipeline coming from new clients, even as 9–24 month sales cycles slow the translation of pipeline into reported revenue.

Fingerprint Cards AB emerges from the call as a company in transition, balancing strong margins and promising AllKey traction against liquidity constraints and execution risk. For investors, the story hinges on whether management can convert a growing and richer pipeline into steady revenue while keeping cash tight and costs under control.
